solution: With \( u=x^{2}+[[k]] \), \( du=2x\,dx \), the limits change from \( x=0,\,[[a]] \) to \( u=[[k]],\,[[a]]^{2}+[[k]] \). The integral becomes \( \dfrac{[[c]]}{2}\displaystyle\int_{[[k]]}^{[[a]]^{2}+[[k]]} u^{[[n]]}\,du = \dfrac{[[c]]}{2}\cdot\dfrac{u^{[[n]]+1}}{[[n]]+1}\bigg|_{[[k]]}^{[[a]]^{2}+[[k]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by 2 to convert \( x\,dx \) into \( \tfrac12 du \).
This doubles the correct value.
This halves the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.

stem: Using \( u = e^{x}+[[k]] \) (so \( du = e^{x}\,dx \)), evaluate \( \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\ln [[b]]} \frac{[[c]]e^{x}}{e^{x}+[[k]]}\,dx \).
solution: With \( u=e^{x}+[[k]] \), \( du=e^{x}\,dx \) exactly matches the numerator, and the limits change from \( x=0,\,\ln [[b]] \) to \( u=1+[[k]],\,[[b]]+[[k]] \). The integral becomes \( [[c]]\displaystyle\int_{1+[[k]]}^{[[b]]+[[k]]} \frac{du}{u} = [[c]]\ln\!\left(\dfrac{[[b]]+[[k]]}{1+[[k]]}\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to shift the limits by \( [[k]] \), using \( u=1 \) and \( u=[[b]] \) instead of \( u=1+[[k]] \) and \( u=[[b]]+[[k]] \).
This keeps only the upper shifted limit and forgets to divide by the lower one inside the log.
This keeps only the lower shifted limit's value rather than the ratio of the two.
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.

stem: Using \( u = \cos x \) (so \( du = -\sin x\,dx \)), evaluate \( \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\pi/2} [[c]]\cos^{[[n]]}x\sin x\,dx \).
solution: With \( u=\cos x \), \( -du=\sin x\,dx \), and the limits change from \( x=0,\,\pi/2 \) to \( u=1,\,0 \). The integral becomes \( -[[c]]\displaystyle\int_{1}^{0} u^{[[n]]}\,du = [[c]]\displaystyle\int_{0}^{1} u^{[[n]]}\,du = \dfrac{[[c]]}{[[n]]+1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( n \) instead of \( n+1 \) in the denominator of the power rule.
This picks up the wrong sign from mishandling the flipped limits after substitution.
This multiplies instead of dividing by \( n+1 \), inverting the power rule.
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.

solution: With \( u=\tan x \), \( du=\sec^{2}x\,dx \), and the limits change from \( x=0,\,\pi/4 \) to \( u=0,\,1 \). The integral becomes \( [[c]]\displaystyle\int_{0}^{1} u^{[[n]]}\,du = \dfrac{[[c]]}{[[n]]+1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports \( n \) itself rather than the power-rule result \( 1/(n+1) \) scaled by \( c \).
This uses \( n+1 \) as a multiplier rather than the correct divisor.
This uses \( n \) instead of \( n+1 \) in the denominator of the power rule.
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.

solution: With \( u=[[k]]x \), \( dx=\dfrac{du}{[[k]]} \), and the limits change from \( x=0,\,[[a]] \) to \( u=0,\,[[k]]\cdot[[a]] \). The integral becomes \( \dfrac{[[c]]}{[[k]]}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{[[k]]\cdot[[a]]} \frac{du}{1+u^{2}} = \dfrac{[[c]]}{[[k]]}\arctan([[k]]\cdot[[a]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by \( [[k]] \) after substituting \( dx=du/[[k]] \).
This multiplies by \( [[k]] \) instead of dividing, the opposite mistake.
This uses \( \arctan([[a]]) \) instead of \( \arctan([[k]]\cdot[[a]]) \), forgetting the substitution changes the arctan's argument too.
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.
